summary: Stranded/behind-the-meter power capacity in the US is an underappreciated resource for AI data centers
summary_extended: Gracias argues that stranded and behind-the-meter power capacity sitting idle in US industrial facilities represents a significantly underappreciated near-term solution to the energy bottleneck constraining AI data center buildout. Rather than waiting years for new power generation capacity to come online, he points to companies like Crusoe Energy that are actively identifying and repurposing unused industrial power infrastructure for AI compute workloads. The investment implication is that this arbitrage between stranded power and AI compute demand is already being acted upon by private companies, suggesting a near-term structural opportunity in alternative data center siting and power sourcing. Crusoe, however, is a private company and is not US-listed, meaning there is no directly actionable public equity implied by this thesis as stated.
detail: Gracias references companies (including Crusoe) finding stranded industrial power capacity across the US and repurposing it for AI compute. He frames this as a near-term solution to the energy bottleneck that doesn't require new generation capacity.
confidence_evidence: Gracias cites specific companies (Crusoe) as examples, suggesting active investment thesis
quote_fragment: stranded capacity in America industrial facilities not being utilized that we can repurpose
